title: Lift gamma_c = N_c·gamma_{c+1} to exact equality gamma_c = N_c (closure step)

comments.author: human
comments.body: Let $N_c=\langle B_c\rangle^F$ be the normal closure of the basic commutators of weight exactly $c$. The easy commutator calculus gives $\gamma_c(F)=N_c\,\gamma_{c+1}(F)$ ($\star$): conjugation $b^g=b[b,g]$ with $[b,g]\in[\gamma_c,F]=\gamma_{c+1}$ shows the image of $N_c$ in $\gamma_c/\gamma_{c+1}$ equals the span of $B_c$, which is ALL of $\gamma_c/\gamma_{c+1}$ by Hall's Basis Theorem.\n\nThe genuine gap: deriving the on-the-nose equality $\gamma_c(F)=N_c$ from $(\star)$. Iterating $(\star)$ with $[N_c,F]\le N_c$ ($N_c$ normal) and $\gamma_{c+1}=[\gamma_c,F]\le N_c\gamma_{c+2}$ yields $\gamma_c\le N_c\gamma_{c+m}$ for all $m$. One then needs $\bigcap_{m}N_c\gamma_{c+m}=N_c$. This is FALSE for general normal subgroups, so the proof must use a special property of $N_c$ (e.g. that $F/N_c$ is residually nilpotent). Residual nilpotence of $F$ alone ($\bigcap_m\gamma_m=1$) does not suffice. This is the mathematical heart of the problem and must be discharged in full.
